participants.reasoning: The extracted description matches the official trial population and key baseline demographics overall, with only minor arm-level differences and no clear contradiction in eligibility or participant characteristics.
participants.extracted: Adults and a small number of older adolescents were screened with suspected ST-elevation acute myocardial infarction, but the randomized trial enrolled 100 patients with acute anterior myocardial infarction who had successful primary PCI within 24 hours of symptom onset and significant anterior wall motion abnormality on left ventricular angiography. Mean age was 56.5 years, 87% were male, and most were Caucasian. Participants were randomized to autologous bone marrow cell infusion (n=55) or placebo (n=45); 92 participants reached the 1-year primary endpoint.
participants.official:
Population: AMI
Age, mean (SD) each arm: placebo group 56.7 (10.7); cell therapy group 56.4 (10.4)
Sex, % male in each arm: placebo 91%; cell therapy 84%
Number of diseased vessels: not reported
Number of stunned hyperkinetic, etc. segments: not reported
Time from symptom onset to initial treatment: placebo 193 min; cell therapy 233 min
Statistically significant baseline imbalances between the groups?: none

interventions.reasoning: The extracted description matches the official intervention details: autologous bone marrow mononuclear cells isolated by density gradient centrifugation and delivered intracoronarily, with only extra timing specifics not contradicting the record.
interventions.extracted: Participants in the intervention arm underwent autologous bone marrow harvest from the iliac crest under local anaesthesia as soon as possible after successful primary percutaneous coronary intervention for acute anterior myocardial infarction (maximum 18 hours after PPCI). The aspirate was processed in a local stem cell laboratory to isolate bone marrow mononuclear cells by density gradient centrifugation, which were washed and resuspended in 10 mL. The resulting autologous bone marrow cell product was then infused intracoronarily into the infarct-related artery in three fractions during stop-flow conditions within 6 hours of harvest.
interventions.official:
Intervention arm: BMSC
Type of stem cells: bone marrow mononuclear cells
Summary of how stem cells were isolated and type and route of delivery: bone marrow aspirate and gradient centrifugation. Following the method set up bySchachinger 2006.
Dose of stem cells: not reported
Timing of stem cell procedure: not reported
Comparator arm: placebo (saline)

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes match the official primary and secondary outcomes in substance, with the same LVEF primary endpoint at 12 months and the same major secondary domains, despite additional detail in the extracted text about imaging sub-measures and UK-only assessments.
outcomes.extracted: Primary outcome: absolute change in global left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF), assessed by advanced cardiac imaging (cardiovascular magnetic resonance or cardiac CT if CMR not feasible), measured at 1 year compared with baseline. Secondary imaging outcomes: change in left ventricular volumes; area at risk (AAR), defined as myocardial oedema mass divided by LV mass; infarct size, defined as infarcted LV mass divided by LV mass; these were assessed from baseline to 3 months and 1 year. Another secondary imaging outcome was change in global ejection fraction by left ventricular angiography at 6 months. Other secondary clinical outcomes: change in plasma NT-proBNP concentration (UK centres only) from baseline to 1 year; New York Heart Association (NYHA) functional class at 6 and 12 months; quality of life scores (European Quality of Life-5 Dimensions index and visual analogue scale; UK centres only) over follow-up through 1 year; and major adverse cardiac events (MACE), including all-cause mortality, recurrent myocardial infarction, and all coronary revascularization, with adverse events monitored through 1 year.
outcomes.official:
Primary outcomes: changes in LVEF from baseline to 12 months (by MRI)
Secondary outcomes: changes in LVEF at 6 months (by echocardiography and LV angiography), major adverse clinical cardiac events, quality of life
Outcome assessment points: baseline, 6 months, 12 months, 5 years
Method(s): MRI, echocardiography and LV angiography, EQL5D index
